class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Learning Analytics and Educational Data Mining
learning_objectives: Define learning analytics and educational data mining
Identify common data sources in educational settings
Apply the learning analytics cycle to an educational problem
readings: Siemens, G. & Baker, R. - Learning Analytics and Educational Data Mining: Towards Communication and Collaboration
Lang, C. et al. - Handbook of Learning Analytics Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Every click, submission, login, and interaction in a digital learning environment generates data. Learning analytics transforms this data into actionable insights that improve teaching and learning.
key_concepts: Learning analytics: measurement, collection, analysis, and reporting of data about learners
Educational data mining: applying data mining techniques to educational data
The learning analytics cycle: learners → data → analysis → intervention → learners
Data sources: LMS logs, assessment data, survey data, SIS records
Levels of analysis: course, program, institution, national
lecture_notes.demonstration: Data exploration: examine a real (anonymized) LMS dataset and identify patterns in student engagement, submission timing, and grade distributions

review.value: Learning analytics focuses on understanding learners and learning contexts to optimize learning; EDM applies computational techniques to discover patterns in educational data. The fields overlap substantially.
Analytics is only valuable when it leads to action: identifying at-risk students is useful only if followed by effective intervention

learning_objectives: Read and interpret common data visualizations
Communicate data findings to non-technical stakeholders
readings: Mandinach, E. & Gummer, E. - Data Literacy for Educators Ch. 1-3
Cairo, A. - How Charts Lie Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational technologists must be able to both analyze data and communicate findings to administrators, faculty, and board members who may not have statistical backgrounds. Data literacy is the bridge between analytics and action.
key_concepts: Types of data visualizations: bar, line, scatter, heatmap, box plot
Common misleading practices: truncated axes, cherry-picked time frames, correlation as causation
Storytelling with data: narrative structure for data presentations
Audience-appropriate communication: dashboards for admins, reports for faculty, interventions for students
Data quality: missing data, outliers, and measurement validity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Chart critique exercise: evaluate five data visualizations from educational reports, identifying misleading elements and suggesting improvements

review.value: A bar chart with a y-axis starting at 70% instead of 0% can make a 5-point difference look like a 50% improvement. Always check axes, sample sizes, and whether the comparison is fair.
Data presentations should follow a narrative: context (why this matters), findings (what the data shows), implications (what should change), and recommendation (what to do next)

learning_objectives: Calculate and interpret descriptive statistics for educational data
Create data visualizations that reveal patterns in student behavior
Apply diagnostic analytics to identify root causes of performance patterns
readings: Practical tutorial: descriptive statistics with spreadsheets

lecture_notes.introduction: Descriptive analytics answers 'what happened?' and diagnostic analytics answers 'why did it happen?' These foundational techniques are the most commonly used and often the most immediately useful.
key_concepts: Central tendency: mean, median, mode and when each is appropriate
Variability: standard deviation, range, interquartile range
Distributions: normal, skewed, bimodal — what each tells us about the data
Cross-tabulation: examining relationships between categorical variables
Visualization: histograms, scatter plots, heatmaps for pattern discovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analytics lab: analyze a course dataset to answer three questions: (1) Are quiz scores normally distributed? (2) Is there a relationship between discussion participation and final grade? (3) Which module has the highest drop-off in content access?

review.value: Use mean when data is normally distributed; use median when data is skewed (as grade data often is). Reporting only the mean can hide bimodal distributions where two groups have very different experiences.
A correlation between discussion posts and grades does not mean discussions cause better grades — higher-performing students may be more motivated to participate in all activities

class_schedule.topic: Predictive Analytics and Early Warning Systems
learning_objectives: Explain how predictive models identify at-risk students
Evaluate the accuracy and fairness of predictive models
Design intervention workflows triggered by predictive analytics
readings: Arnold, K. & Pistilli, M. - Course Signals at Purdue: Using Learning Analytics to Increase Student Success
Jayaprakash, S. et al. - Early Alert of Academically At-Risk Students

lecture_notes.introduction: Predictive analytics uses historical patterns to forecast future outcomes. In education, this typically means identifying students at risk of failing early enough to intervene. The promise is real, but so are the ethical risks.
key_concepts: Logistic regression for pass/fail prediction
Feature selection: which variables predict success?
Model evaluation: accuracy, precision, recall, and the cost of false positives vs. false negatives
Purdue's Course Signals: the pioneering early alert system
Fairness: does the model perform equally well across demographic groups?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a simple predictive model: using a spreadsheet, create a weighted risk score based on login frequency, assignment completion, and quiz performance, then test it against actual outcomes

review.value: Purdue's system used traffic-light indicators (green/yellow/red) based on predictive models. Studies showed improved outcomes, though methodology debates persist about selection effects.

A false positive (flagging a student who would have passed) wastes intervention resources and may stigmatize. A false negative (missing a student who fails) misses the whole point. The cost asymmetry must guide threshold setting.

class_schedule.topic: Ethics, Privacy, and Governance in Learning Analytics
learning_objectives: Apply ethical frameworks to learning analytics decisions
Design data governance policies for educational institutions
Evaluate the tension between analytics benefits and student privacy
readings: Slade, S. & Prinsloo, P. - Learning Analytics: Ethical Issues and Dilemmas
JISC - Code of Practice for Learning Analytics

lecture_notes.introduction: Just because we can collect and analyze student data does not mean we should in every case. Learning analytics raises fundamental questions about privacy, consent, power, and the right to be imperfect.
key_concepts: Consent and transparency: do students know what data is collected and how it is used?
Purpose limitation: data collected for learning support should not be repurposed for marketing
Algorithmic accountability: who is responsible when a predictive model makes an error?
Student agency: students should have access to and control over their own data
JISC Code of Practice: a framework for ethical learning analytics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy drafting workshop: draft a learning analytics governance policy for a university, addressing consent, access, retention, and accountability

review.value: Should a poor first-year GPA follow a student through predictive models for four years? Students grow and change. Analytics systems need mechanisms for data decay and student redemption.

Institutions analyze student data from a position of power. Students typically cannot opt out without opting out of the learning environment itself. This asymmetry demands strong governance protections.

learning_objectives: Design effective learning analytics dashboards for different audiences
Apply data visualization best practices to educational data
Present analytics findings with actionable recommendations
readings: Few, S. - Information Dashboard Design Ch. 3-5
Verbert, K. et al. - Learning Dashboards: An Overview and Future Research Opportunities

lecture_notes.introduction: The most sophisticated analysis is useless if decision-makers cannot understand or act on it. Dashboard design and data communication are critical skills for the educational data analyst.
key_concepts: Dashboard design principles: focus, simplicity, actionability
Audience-specific dashboards: instructor, student, administrator, accreditor
Real-time vs. retrospective dashboards
Alert design: when to push notifications vs. pull reports
From insight to action: embedding recommendations in dashboards
lecture_notes.demonstration: Dashboard design exercise: create mockups for three dashboards (student self-tracking, instructor early warning, dean's program overview) for the same underlying dataset
setup_requirements: Dashboard mockup tool (Figma, Google Slides, or paper)
Sample dataset for all three audiences

review.value: Every data point on a dashboard should answer the question 'so what?' If an instructor sees that 30% of students haven't logged in this week, the dashboard should offer 'send reminder email' as an action, not just display the number.
Student-facing dashboards should support self-regulation: show progress toward goals, highlight areas needing attention, and suggest next steps — not just display grades

quizzes.topic: Learning Analytics Foundations and Data Literacy

questions.question: A course has 200 students with a mean final grade of 78% and a standard deviation of 15%. The grade distribution is bimodal with peaks at 65% and 90%. Why would reporting only the mean be misleading, and what additional information should be provided?
questions.answer: The mean of 78% suggests a single, centered distribution of performance, obscuring the fact that students cluster into two distinct groups. One group is struggling (around 65%) and another is performing well (around 90%). Reporting only the mean would miss the opportunity to identify and support the lower-performing group. Additional information: report the median, the two modal peaks, the distribution shape (bimodal), and ideally a histogram showing the two clusters. The bimodal pattern may indicate different subpopulations (e.g., students with vs. without prerequisite knowledge) that need different interventions.

rubric.key: Explains why the mean misrepresents bimodal data
Identifies what additional statistics to report
Suggests practical implications of the bimodal pattern

questions.question: You find a strong positive correlation (r = 0.72) between number of discussion posts and final course grade. A colleague concludes that requiring more discussion posts will improve grades. Evaluate this conclusion.
questions.answer: This conclusion commits the correlation-causation fallacy. Alternative explanations: (1) Self-selection — motivated, high-performing students may choose to post more because they are already engaged, not because posting causes their success. (2) Third variable — underlying student motivation or time availability may cause both more posting and higher grades. (3) Reverse causation — students who are performing well may feel more confident participating in discussions. Requiring more posts could lead to low-quality, compliance-driven participation that does not improve learning. A better approach: design discussion activities that promote deep engagement and test whether quality (not quantity) of participation predicts outcomes.

rubric.key: Provides at least two alternative explanations
Suggests a better approach to using this finding

questions.question: A predictive model flags a student as 'high risk' of failure based on low login frequency. The student is a single parent who downloads all materials weekly and studies offline. Explain the failure mode of this model and propose a design improvement.
questions.answer: The model fails because it uses login frequency as a proxy for engagement, assuming that online activity equals learning. This student is engaged but invisible to the model because their learning happens offline. This is a construct validity problem: the model measures online behavior, not learning. Design improvement: (1) Include assignment submission and grade data as engagement indicators, not just login data. (2) Weight outcome-based features (quiz scores, assignment grades) more heavily than behavior-based features (logins, page views). (3) Allow students to self-report their study patterns. (4) Before triggering interventions, have an advisor review the full picture including assignment performance.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the proxy measurement problem
Explains why this specific student is misclassified

questions.question: Describe three key principles from the JISC Code of Practice for Learning Analytics that address the power asymmetry between institutions and students.
questions.answer: (1) Transparency: institutions must clearly communicate what data is collected, how it is analyzed, and what decisions it informs. Students should not be subject to invisible algorithmic judgments. (2) Student access and control: students should be able to view their own analytics data and have mechanisms to correct inaccuracies. They should also understand how to interpret the data. (3) Consent and opt-out: while institutions may collect data for legitimate educational purposes, students should have the ability to opt out of non-essential analytics without academic penalty. These principles address power asymmetry by giving students visibility, agency, and voice in how their data is used.

rubric.key: Explains how each addresses power asymmetry
Demonstrates understanding of student agency in analytics

major_assignments.title: Learning Analytics Investigation and Dashboard Design
major_assignments.description: Analyze a provided educational dataset (anonymized LMS and grade data for a multi-section course). Produce: (1) a descriptive analytics report identifying key patterns, (2) a diagnostic analysis of performance gaps, (3) a simple predictive risk model, (4) dashboard mockups for instructor and student audiences, and (5) an ethics assessment of your analytics approach.
objectives: Conduct descriptive and diagnostic analytics on real educational data
Build and evaluate a simple predictive model
Apply ethical principles to analytics decisions
deliverables: Spreadsheet with descriptive statistics and predictive model
Presentation slides summarizing findings and recommendations

criteria: Appropriate statistical measures chosen and correctly calculated
Effective data visualizations that reveal patterns
Model evaluated for accuracy and fairness
Actionable design with clear next steps
Identifies specific ethical risks in the analysis
Honest assessment of limitations and ethical implications

quizzes.description: Bi-weekly quizzes on analytics concepts, methods, and ethics

lab_assignments.description: Weekly analytics labs: data cleaning, visualization, statistical analysis, dashboard prototyping

major_assignments.description: Learning analytics investigation and dashboard design

midterm_examination.description: Covers descriptive statistics, data literacy, and visualization

final_project.description: Comprehensive analytics project with predictive modeling and stakeholder presentation

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions are mandatory. Analytics skills are built through practice. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the final grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late work loses 5% per day for up to 5 days. Dataset access may expire; complete work within the access window.
course_policies.collaboration: Study groups for analytics labs are encouraged. All submitted analyses, reports, and dashboards must be individually produced.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All analyses must be your own work. Data must be handled ethically — never attempt to de-anonymize datasets. Violations result in a zero and referral.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Computer with spreadsheet software (Excel or Google Sheets). Optional but recommended: R or Python for advanced analytics. Data visualization tool (Tableau Public, Google Data Studio, or similar).
